articles.full_text:
Arunachal Battles Relentless Floods; IAF Airlifts 9, Red Alert Continues
First Published: 29th June, 2026 13:53 IST
Indian Air Force helicopters conducted aerial surveys over the affected area, helping rescue teams quickly locate the stranded individuals
The flood situation in Arunachal Pradesh continues to remain grim as incessant heavy rainfall has triggered flash floods and landslides across several districts, severely disrupting normal life. Amid the worsening crisis, the Indian Air Force successfully rescued all nine persons who had gone missing after a rescue boat was swept away by the swollen Sisiri River in Lower Dibang Valley district. Meanwhile, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has warned of thunderstorms accompanied by heavy to very heavy rainfall at isolated places across the state till July 2.
The rescue operation was launched after an inflatable rubber boat carrying seven members of a rescue team and three persons who had already been rescued was overpowered by the strong currents of the Sisiri River, which is in high spate due to continuous rainfall. The boat capsized, prompting an extensive search operation early Sunday.
Indian Air Force helicopters conducted aerial surveys over the affected area, helping rescue teams quickly locate the stranded individuals. Earlier, seven of the nine missing persons were rescued, while the remaining two were later traced and brought back safely. The rescued persons include the Gaon Burah, or village headman, of Dambuk. Local MLA Punnyo Apum remained at the site throughout the operation, monitoring the rescue efforts until all nine people were safely evacuated.
While relief came in Lower Dibang Valley, the flood situation worsened in Leparada and East Siang districts following heavy rainfall. Authorities have sounded a high alert and begun evacuating residents from vulnerable low-lying areas. Three families have already been shifted to safer locations in East Siang.
The latest developments come even as relief and rescue operations continue in Keyi Panyor district, where earlier flash floods caused widespread devastation. Heavy rains have continued to lash Leparada and East Siang since morning, prompting the administration to issue a red alert.
The situation is particularly alarming in Leparada, where all rivers and streams are flowing above the danger mark. Electricity supply has been disrupted in several areas, while landslides triggered by the heavy downpour have severely affected road connectivity.
In East Siang district, a massive landslide has blocked the flow of the Siji River, raising fears of further flooding downstream. Red alert has been sounded across the district as incessant rainfall since Saturday night has caused severe flooding in rivers and rivulets, damaging roads, electric poles, agricultural fields, residential houses and other public infrastructure.
The Sibo Korong River, which flows through Pasighat township, has washed away portions of local roads, disrupted transportation, and caused extensive riverbank erosion. The swollen river, carrying strong currents and debris, has also damaged several dwelling houses and other properties.
A section of National Highway-13 between Raneghat Bridge and Ayeng village was also threatened after sudden torrents carrying heavy debris flowed down from the hills through an area that had no previous river course, creating panic among commuters travelling towards Mebo Sub-Division and the eastern parts of Arunachal Pradesh.
However, timely intervention by Zila Parishad Member Bomken Tayeng helped avert a major disruption. Deploying heavy earth-moving machinery and workers, Tayeng diverted the sudden floodwaters through an existing culvert, preventing the highway from being washed away.
In another instance, landslide debris blocked sections of the Raneghat-Bodak road. Heavy earth movers deployed by Mebo MLA Oken Tayeng cleared the road, restoring connectivity to Upper Siang district. Meanwhile, another stretch of the Pasighat-Ledum-Tene road near Remi village under Bilat Block was badly damaged by sudden floodwaters.
Residents across the affected districts have urged the government to restore damaged roads on a war footing, establish relief camps, and implement long-term flood protection measures to reduce future risks.
Keeping in view the prevailing weather conditions and extensive damage caused by the rains, the East Siang district administration has ordered all schools to remain closed on June 29 and 30. Officials continue to monitor the situation closely, while residents living near vulnerable riverbanks and landslide-prone areas have been advised to remain alert until water levels recede.
Meanwhile, search operations are continuing at Yazali in Keyi Panyor district, where the third body recovered from the Panyor River near Hawa Camp village in Kimin Circle has been identified as Saurav Kumar, who had gone missing in the flash floods. With this, the number of bodies recovered in the Neepco Colony flash flood tragedy has risen to three, while two more persons remain missing.
According to the rainfall data recorded during the last 24 hours, Pasighat in East Siang district received 152 millimetres of rainfall. Kangku in Lower Siang recorded 31.5 millimetres, Itanagar in Papum Pare district also received 31.5 millimetres, Yingkiong in Upper Siang recorded 38.8 millimetres, while Along in West Siang received 40 millimetres of rainfall.
The India Meteorological Department has forecast thunderstorms accompanied by heavy to very heavy rainfall at isolated places across Arunachal Pradesh till July 2, urging authorities and residents to remain vigilant as rescue, relief and restoration efforts continue across the flood-hit state.
